What it does: Rust "Context Operating System". Intercepts terminal commands, streams output through declarative TOML rule-based distillers (no LLM calls). Adaptive compression self-tunes based on omni_retrieve frequency. Smart bypass skips distillation under 2000-token outputs. RewindStore: zero info loss, raw output retrievable via omni_retrieve. Native MCP server (omni mcp, stdio, no subprocess latency). Engrams: automatic rule-based subtask digests (no LLM cost) preventing context amnesia. Context pressure traffic light (omni_insight, warns at 65%/82% thresholds). Goal-driven constraint scaling (debug retains errors, refactor compresses code). Multi-agent collaboration via OMNI_AGENT_ID — Cursor/Claude/Hermes share filtered memory streams. Session handoffs (omni_handoff). Lightweight dependency graph at hook time. v0.6.0, 42 releases, migrated from Node/Zig to Rust.
Conflict / Overlap: Either/or with RTK, sqz, lean-ctx — pick exactly one shell tool. Engrams/session memory overlaps lean-ctx CCP, Bitloops, claude-mem (cross-session memory) — different mechanism: omni is rule-based, no LLM cost. Context pressure system overlaps token-optimizer session health monitoring. omni_handoff overlaps Aura and codemap cross-agent handoff.
Requirements: Rust binary; no model calls (rule-based TOML distillers, not AI compression); brew install fajarhide/tap/omni or curl installer
Activity (Issues / PRs): 26 forks, 42 releases, v0.6.0 Jun 14 2026, 638 commits, active — organic community discussion comparing directly to RTK
Verdict: Add — sophisticated shell tool alternative, no model dependency
Decision Rule: 259 stars but real organic adoption (Indonesian dev community comparing directly to RTK). No LLM calls for core mechanism — same "zero model dependency" category as RTK/lean-ctx, unlike headroom or claude-mem. Unique: rule-based Engrams (free subtask memory), context pressure traffic light, goal-driven compression scaling. brew install fajarhide/tap/omni then omni init --claude. Author also maintains Heimsense (separate tool to run Claude Code on non-Anthropic models) — worth knowing exists.

What it does: Six PowerShell scripts the agent calls instead of raw commands to reduce context bloat. run-compact.ps1: runs noisy builds/tests, feeds only error summary to agent (-93% terminal lines). read-symbol.ps1: grabs only a named function/class/component by name instead of reading whole file. read-window.ps1: reads a precise line range. investigate.ps1: batches multiple search patterns into one structured report (-71% shell commands). find-in-file.ps1: fast literal search in known file. diff-summary.ps1: ultra-compact git status. Agent rules in AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md instruct agent to use these scripts automatically. PowerShell 7.0+. v0.1.1, 14 commits.
Conflict / Overlap: Stackable with RTK/sqz/lean-ctx/omni (not either/or): those auto-intercept ALL shell output; agent-context-economy scripts are explicitly called by the agent for targeted reads and runs. No MCP tool name conflicts. read-symbol.ps1 overlaps conceptually with pharos-mcp/Serena symbol lookup but different mechanism (PowerShell script vs MCP server).
Requirements: PowerShell 7.0+ required. Windows-first (works on macOS/Linux via PowerShell Core but tooling is Windows-oriented). No model calls. git clone then run setup-ai-scripts.ps1, then copy agent rules from examples/AGENTS.example.md into CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 2 releases, v0.1.1 Jul 2 2026, 14 commits, solo dev — brand new
Verdict: Watch — too early, Windows-first constraint, explicit-call model
Decision Rule: 2 stars, v0.1.1 released yesterday. Windows/PowerShell-first is a real constraint for macOS/Linux-primary environments. Explicit-call model (agent must be instructed to use scripts) vs automatic hooks (RTK/omni). Stackable rather than competing with shell hooks. Benchmarks (93% terminal reduction, 90% file read reduction) lack methodology detail — treat as illustrative. Note: kayba-ai/agentic-context-engine is also abbreviated ACE — different tool entirely.

What it does: Token optimizer spanning OpenCode, Cursor, Claude Code, and Claude Desktop in one project. Techniques: quieter Bash flags + blocked low-signal reads before a tool call, output compression + symbol-only file reads after, duplicate/stale context pruning before each request, BM25-based relevant-file selection (trim_context/smart_read MCP tools), conciseness instructions for model output. Per-platform automatic-vs-on-demand disclosed explicitly: OpenCode fully automatic (plugin + sidebar TUI widget); Claude Code automatic via PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks + on-demand MCP; Cursor pre-call rewrite automatic only (platform hook API cannot rewrite output for built-in tools, so output compression has no automatic path); Claude Desktop MCP-only (no hook/plugin API). .ctxliteignore (gitignore-style) blocks project-specific paths from reads/trim_context. 4 packages: @ctxlite/opencode, @ctxlite/mcp, @ctxlite/core (shared BM25 + tree-sitter symbol extraction), @ctxlite/cli. Unified stats at ~/.ctxlite/stats.db across all tools/platforms — npx @ctxlite/cli stats --last 7d --export json. Legacy Go HTTP proxy (L1/L2 caching) explicitly superseded by current plugin/MCP/hooks architecture. 74 commits, 7 tags.
Conflict / Overlap: ⛔ HARD (shell layer): same hook position as RTK/sqz/lean-ctx/omni — pick exactly one shell/output tool. get_stats/trim_context/smart_read soft-overlap tokentelemetry (observability) and code-nav BM25 retrieval tools conceptually, but no hard MCP tool name collision found.
Requirements: Zero-LLM; no model calls — BM25 + tree-sitter symbol extraction only. Node.js required for npx installers. npx @ctxlite/cli install --tool <opencode|claude-code|cursor|claude-desktop> --scope global --yes, or npx @ctxlite/cli install for interactive picker.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 7 tags, 74 commits, CI passing, solo/small team — no third-party coverage found, genuinely very early
Verdict: Watch — too early, but unusually honest capability disclosure and clean architecture
Decision Rule: Per-platform automatic-vs-on-demand matrix in the README is unusually candid — explicitly states where Cursor's own hook API prevents automatic output compression, rather than implying uniform behaviour across platforms. Legacy Go proxy left in the repo with an explicit "superseded" label rather than silently deleted — good transparency about architectural history. Licence: Commons Clause only restricts reselling/rebranding ctxlite itself as a competing product; commercial/internal company use is fully free. Pre-Commons-Clause versions (v0.1.0-v0.1.25) remain permanently plain MIT, not retroactively changed. Cross-platform breadth (4 tools in one project) is uncommon among shell-compression tools in this comparison, most of which target 1-2 platforms.

What it does: MCP server replacing standard Claude Code file tools with cached/compressed equivalents. 61 tools: smart_read (serves diffs after first read, ~80% reduction on repeated reads), smart_grep, smart_glob, optimize_text (cache large content externally to remove from context), cache_warmup (SQLite pre-warm on session start), get_session_stats, monitoring dashboard. SQLite-based persistent cross-session cache. Brotli compression (2-4x typical, up to 82x repetitive content). Claims: 60-90% token reduction across 38,000+ operations. v5.0.1, 31 releases.
Conflict / Overlap: ⚠ 61 MCP tool definitions = significant schema overhead at session start — the problem this comparison is solving. Must run with Tool Search deferral to avoid defeating own purpose. smart_read/smart_grep conceptually overlap context-mode (all tool output) and headroom (universal compression) but different mechanism (file-level SQLite cache vs stream compression).
Requirements: ⚠ Node.js 18+. npm install -g @ooples/token-optimizer-mcp (post-install hook auto-installs hooks). HIGH severity unfixed npm vulnerability (picomatch, May 2026 scan). No external API calls — local SQLite cache only.

Verdict: Watch — dormant since Nov 2025; unpatched HIGH severity npm vulnerability; README deleted
Decision Rule: Project appears abandoned since Nov 2025 (7+ months). README.md replaced with CI/CD workflow docs — actual product documentation is gone from the repo. SkillsLLM automated security scan (May 2026) flagged HIGH severity picomatch vulnerability unfixed. The persistent SQLite cross-session file cache is a genuinely unique capability not replicated elsewhere in this comparison — revisit if project resumes. Must use alongside Tool Search deferral to avoid 61-tool schema overhead problem.

What it does: Unifies every context source under one URI scheme and one verb set. Two skills: mfs-ingest (mfs add registers/syncs any source), mfs-find (search, grep, ls, cat, tree, head, tail across what is ingested). Connectors: local files, S3/R2/GCS/MinIO, Google Drive, Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, BigQuery, Snowflake, GitHub (files+issues+PRs), Jira, Linear, HubSpot, Zendesk, Slack, Discord, Gmail, Feishu, Notion, crawled web. --all fans one query across every registered source. Default stack is fully local: ONNX embedding (BGE-M3 int8, ~600MB one-time download, no API key/GPU/cloud), Milvus Lite (local file), SQLite metadata — scales to self-hosted Milvus or Zilliz Cloud + Postgres for production. Credential TOMLs hold only env:/file: references, never raw secrets — real values resolve server-side. 70+ agent integrations via npx skills add. Python/TS/Rust SDKs for building on top. v0.4.3 (installer), 146 commits. 3rd iteration of a Zilliz lineage (claude-context, memsearch).
Conflict / Overlap: Broadest-scope tool in this comparison — spans personal knowledge retrieval, library docs, tabular data, and agent memory persistence simultaneously rather than one category. Does not compete with single-purpose tools (qmd, jdatamunch-mcp, graphthulhu) so much as offer to subsume them under one namespace. No MCP server currently — skills + CLI + SDK only, unlike most other tools in this comparison.
Requirements: Local mode: no API key, no GPU, no cloud account — ~600MB ONNX model auto-downloaded on first run. Optional: OpenAI/Gemini/Voyage/Ollama embedding providers, Zilliz Cloud or self-hosted Milvus, Postgres. uv tool install mfs-server or cargo install mfs-cli, or npx skills add zilliztech/mfs --all -g for agent skill install.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 2 forks, 5 releases, v0.4.3 (latest installer)/v0.4.1 (page), 146 commits, backed by Zilliz (Milvus vector DB company) — genuinely early despite company backing
Verdict: Add — broadest-scope context unification tool here, genuinely local-first, but very early
Decision Rule: Only tool in this comparison trying to unify code + memory + docs + chat + tickets + databases + cloud storage under one interface. Default stack is fully local and offline (ONNX + Milvus Lite + SQLite) — no forced cloud dependency unlike supermemory or githits-cli, though Zilliz Cloud is available to scale. Credential design is notably careful: TOML never holds raw secrets. 4 stars reflects genuine novelty/earliness, not a red flag — backed by the same team behind Milvus. No MCP server yet (skills/CLI/SDK only) — watch for that to land given every other new tool in this comparison ships one.

What it does: Version-aware index of the entire public open-source ecosystem, hosted on GitHits infrastructure (Rust indexer, Elixir coordination, Python agentic layer). Local MCP server + CLI let agents search/read/grep the exact source of any npm/PyPI/Crates/NuGet/Maven/Go/RubyGems/Hex/Packagist package at the installed version, without cloning. get_example: cross-repository prior-art search ("HTTP retries with exponential backoff in Python") sourced from repos, issues, discussions, PRs. pkg_vulns, pkg_deps, pkg_changelog, pkg_upgrade_review for dependency evidence. docs_list/docs_read for hosted+repo-backed documentation. License filtering: strict (default, excludes copyleft/undeclared) / custom (account blocklist) / yolo (disabled). 19 supported coding tools including Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI. v0.6.1, 48 releases, 516 commits.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts with existing tools. Closest to context (neuledge) and jdocmunch-mcp (library documentation retrieval) but broader scope: indexes the entire public OSS ecosystem on GitHits own infrastructure rather than pre-packaged libraries or self-indexed local docs.
Requirements: ⚠ SaaS-hosted — requires GitHits account + OAuth login; all indexing runs on GitHits infrastructure, CLI/MCP calls their API. Node.js ^20.12.0 or >=22.13.0. npx githits@latest init. Forever-free tier available; credit-based for indexed search beyond free tier.

Decision Rule: Only tool in this comparison indexing the entire public OSS ecosystem rather than your own repo or a pre-packaged library set. get_example cross-repository prior-art search has no equivalent elsewhere here. Founders: opencv-python creator (100M+ downloads) as CTO, ex-Smartly chief architect. Credentials stored in system keychain; GITHITS_API_TOKEN for CI/headless. Does not access private repos. Forever-free tier exists but core value requires an ongoing account relationship with GitHits — not a self-hosted or one-time-install tool like most others in this comparison.

What it does: CLI tool making file-to-file relationships an explicit, declared part of project configuration rather than agent-inferred. depmesh.toml defines relations (tested_by, governed_by, imports, imported_by, or any custom relation) via glob pattern rules, fixed lists, CLI commands, or scripts. depmesh dependencies --relation tested_by ./src/app.py returns exact related artifacts deterministically — no AST parsing, no embeddings, no LLM inference. depmesh skill usage/configuration/initialization give agent-oriented docs. Self-dogfoods its own depmesh.toml. BSD-3-Clause, Python 97.1%.

Fundamentally different mechanism from codegraph/GitNexus/CodeGraphContext (all infer structure via AST/embeddings) — depmesh requires explicit rule configuration, trading setup effort for determinism. Narrower than lat.md (no documentation-authoring component, just relation queries).
Requirements: Zero-LLM; no model calls anywhere — pure deterministic pattern matching. Python via uv or pip. uv tool install depmesh or pip install depmesh, then depmesh init to scaffold starter config.

Only useful once depmesh.toml is meaningfully configured for the project; the starter config from depmesh init is just a template. Trades manual relation-definition effort for zero hallucination risk on "what tests cover this file"-style queries. Worth revisiting once community adoption signals appear.

What it does: Structural code bookmarking system using tree-sitter to capture semantic structure rather than fragile file:line coordinates. Bookmarks self-heal across renames, refactors, and formatting changes via tiered matching (Exact → Relaxed → Hash Fallback). codemark add --file src/auth.rs --range 42 --tag auth --note "JWT entry point" bookmarks the AST node, not the line number. codemark resolve <id> finds it even after the code moved. Semantic search via local vector embeddings (sqlite-vec, no API key): codemark reindex then codemark search --semantic "how are tokens validated?". Collections group bookmarks into task-specific sets. Git-integrated: tracks bookmarks across commits/branches. Integrations: Television (TUI fuzzy-finder), Neovim (gutter signs, visual-selection bookmarking, Telescope), Claude Code (dedicated plugin, badged "agent-ready"). 8 languages via tree-sitter: Rust, Swift, TypeScript/TSX, Python, Go, Java, Dart, C#. SQLite bundled. 112 commits, 10 releases, v0.5.2 (Apr 20 2026), published on crates.io.
Conflict / Overlap: Different mechanism from the code-nav cluster (codegraph/GitNexus/tokensave/Serena/pharos-mcp/CodeGraphContext/jCodemunch): those answer "what is related to this code" via live graph query or symbol resolution; codemark answers "does the location I bookmarked earlier still exist, and where" via self-healing structural pointers. Complementary, not competing — same relationship depmesh has to this layer. No MCP server visible in what was fetched; appears to be CLI + Claude Code plugin (skill/hook pattern) rather than MCP tools, so no hard name-collision risk with existing MCP-based code nav tools.
Requirements: Zero-LLM; semantic search uses local vector embeddings (sqlite-vec) — no API key required, confirmed explicitly in README. Rust 1.75+ to build from source; SQLite bundled. brew tap DanielCardonaRojas/codemark && brew install codemark, or cargo install codemark.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 10 releases, v0.5.2 Apr 20 2026, 112 commits, published on crates.io, author has 65 GitHub repos — technically mature but genuinely undiscovered, no third-party coverage found
Verdict: Watch — too early, but a genuinely novel mechanism not replicated elsewhere in this comparison
Decision Rule: Self-healing structural bookmarks (tiered Exact → Relaxed → Hash Fallback resolution) solve pointer decay — a real problem for long-running agent sessions where code shifts between turns and a raw file:line reference silently breaks. Claude Code plugin is purpose-built for this: agent bookmarks a location, retrieves it later by semantic meaning rather than needing to remember exact coordinates. 0 stars despite 112 commits and 10 releases through v0.5.2 — technically mature, just undiscovered. Also has genuine human-facing value via Neovim and Television TUI integrations, not purely agent-oriented.

What it does: Repo-local continuity runtime. Solves agent cold-start: each session starts from the operational state left by prior work via two CLI calls — aictx resume (produces resume capsule with active task, next action, known failures, verified strategies, branch-safe Work State) and aictx finalize (captures execution evidence). Core capabilities: Work State (task, hypothesis, files, next action, risks), Failure Memory (structured patterns of observed command/test/build/lint failures — agents avoid repeating them), Strategy Memory (reusable successful execution patterns), Handoffs/Decisions (survive session boundaries), RepoMap (optional Tree-sitter structural map). Claude-aware (.claude/hooks/aictx_*.py), Codex-first, generic-agent compatible. All artifacts local JSON/Markdown in .aictx/ directory. No model calls. 28 releases, v5.3.0. Demo: 5 vs 10 files explored, 8 vs 15 commands, 1m12s vs 1m59s (honest framing: not a universal benchmark).
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts — CLI-only (no MCP server). Overlaps cross-session governance layer (Bitloops, Aura, graphiti) but different emphasis: AICTX captures operational state (task/failure/strategy) not reasoning chains (Bitloops) or temporal fact graphs (graphiti). Uniquely tracks structured failure patterns — no other tool here explicitly prevents agents from repeating known failed commands.
Requirements: Python 3.10+; no model calls; no external API needed. pip install aictx then aictx install then aictx init. RepoMap uses Tree-sitter (optional, pip extra). Works offline.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 1 fork, 28 releases, v5.3.0 May 4 2026, 137 commits, solo dev (Santi Santamaria), official website at aictx.org
Verdict: Watch — 8 stars but 28 releases; unique failure memory; needs community validation
Decision Rule: 8 stars but 28 releases from v1 to v5.3.0 suggests sustained personal development before publicizing. README explicitly rules out productivity guarantees and lists what it is not — good epistemic hygiene. Failure Memory is unique in this comparison: structured tracking of commands/tests that failed, with dedupe and staleness metadata. What it does: Graph-structured memory of engineering knowledge (components, flows, decisions, constraints) for coding agents. Two skills: greplica-bootstrap (once, repo start) and greplica-update-working-memory (end of sessions with durable decisions). greplica graph context "<question>" retrieves relevant prior context before broad manual exploration — reduces repeated rg searches and rediscovery of past decisions. Retrieval combines keyword search, semantic embeddings, and graph traversal. Local embedding mode available (no API key) or OpenAI embeddings. Supports Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, GitHub Copilot CLI (Cline in progress). Stored at ~/.greplica/graph.db, no cloud sync, no telemetry. Benchmarked on SWE-chat (public dataset + paper): held-out planning tasks, clean base snapshot + 2-4 prior sessions build context, baseline vs Greplica-context run compared. Best case 75% fewer tokens + 38% faster; typical 40-50% token reduction. Company-backed (Autoloops).
Conflict / Overlap: Overlaps aictx (cross-session governance) — different model: aictx tracks operational state (task/failure/strategy), Greplica tracks a claims/sources/edges knowledge graph (architecture-document-like). Also overlaps gitmem/memoir conceptually (persistent engineering memory) but positioned around planning-task benchmarks specifically, not multi-tool sharing or git branching.
Requirements: Node.js + npm. Native npm packages (better-sqlite3) need local build tools. Local embedding mode: no API key required. OpenAI embedding mode: OPENAI_API_KEY needed for graph context search and proposal application. npm install -g greplica then greplica install --platform <claude|codex|opencode> --embedding local.

Token tracking is estimate-based (~15% accuracy). v1.0.4 — things may break.

What it does: Markdown knowledge graph for codebases stored in a lat.md/ directory. Interconnected .md files document architecture, business logic, design decisions, and test specs — linked via [[wiki links]] to each other and to source code symbols (// @lat: [[section-id]] in code). Agents navigate via lat search (semantic), lat section, lat locate, lat refs, lat expand (expands [[refs]] inline without loading full graph). lat check enforces referential integrity between docs and code. Test spec enforcement: require-code-mention: true flags specs without code backlinks. lat mcp starts MCP server. lat init scaffolds directory and installs hooks in Claude Code / Codex / OpenCode. Agents maintain the graph; humans review semantic diffs in lat.md/ before code diffs. 21 releases, v0.11.0 Mar 2026. Author: Yury Selivanov (Python asyncio, uvloop, EdgeDB).
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts. Occupies a distinct niche from sigmap (signatures → CLAUDE.md), codesight (routes/schema → CLAUDE.md), codemap (budget-aware injection). lat.md creates a navigable knowledge graph rather than pushing content to CLAUDE.md. Complements sigmap: sigmap handles what code does, lat.md handles why and how architecture decisions were made.
Requirements: Node.js 22+, pnpm. lat search requires OpenAI API key (sk-...) or Vercel AI Gateway key (vck_...) for semantic search — lat locate/section/refs/check work without it. npm install -g lat.md then lat init.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 106 forks, 21 releases, v0.11.0 Mar 23 2026, 191 commits, 13 open issues, 14 open PRs, HN front page discussion Mar 29 2026
Verdict: Add — unique architecture documentation governance; dormant since Mar 2026
Decision Rule: Only tool here addressing AGENTS.md scalability: replaces a single flat file with a linked graph of .md files. lat check enforces bidirectional links between docs and code, catching drift that ctxharness misses (ctxharness checks version facts; lat.md checks structural links). Author Yury Selivanov (Python asyncio/uvloop/EdgeDB) gives strong credibility. Dormant since March 25, 2026 — 3 months without commits. Semantic search requires OpenAI key; all other features work offline.

What it does: Symbol Card graph (LadybugDB) with 4-rung Iris Gate Ladder: escalate from 100-token Symbol Card → Skeleton IR → Hot-Path Excerpt → policy-gated raw source. Live indexing (unsaved edits). Graph slicing with weighted BFS. SCIP compiler-grade cross-refs. Cross-session development memories. Tool gateway (38→6 tools). Sandboxed runtime execution (outputMode: minimal). Observability dashboard. v0.11.3.
Conflict / Overlap: Overlaps GitNexus (symbol graph + blast radius + Claude Code integration) and codebase-memory-mcp (graph queries). Iris Gate Ladder, live indexing, SCIP, and tool gateway are unique. Tool gateway directly reduces MCP definition token overhead.
Requirements: ⚠ Node.js 24+ required; no external model calls; SCIP optional for compiler-grade edges
Activity (Issues / PRs): 12+ open issues (incl. FD leak #12: 240k+ FDs after 18min use), 40 releases, 794 commits, active
Verdict: Add — best-in-class controlled escalation
Decision Rule: Source-available: free for personal/internal use. ⚠ Known file descriptor leak (issue #12) — 240k+ FDs after 18min use in Claude Code sessions. Iris Gate Ladder is most sophisticated token-minimisation mechanism in this table. Requires Node.js 24+.

What it does: Claude Code skill that dispatches 6 parallel audit agents across your full config stack: CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, skills, MCP servers, commands, rules, .claudeignore. Measures per-message overhead, identifies what is eating context, implements fixes with backup + diff approval. Skill usage analytics (tracks which of your 59 installed skills you actually use). Session health monitoring. Interactive HTML dashboard. Python measurement script with before/after comparison. Local SQLite trend tracking. No releases yet.
Conflict / Overlap: No overlap with other tools — unique diagnostic layer. Every other tool reduces context during sessions; token-optimizer reduces the fixed per-message baseline before any session starts.
Requirements: Python 3.10+ + Claude Code skill; ⚠ dispatches 6 Claude Code sub-agents (uses your Claude session); PolyForm NC
Activity (Issues / PRs): 1.4k stars (was ~1k), 16 waste patterns, 3 tiers, OpenClaw + Hermes + Codex support added, security: PASSED
Verdict: Add — unique diagnostic layer
Decision Rule: SkillsLLM security audit PASSED (May 2026). Now supports OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex in addition to Claude Code. | Nothing else here audits the Claude Code config stack itself. PolyForm NC: free for personal use, commercial needs licence. Install via Claude Code plugin marketplace. Most valuable for power users with many MCP servers and skills.

What it does: CLI/CI tool that validates AI instruction files (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules) for drift and quality. L1 fact drift: version numbers in docs vs actual package.json/Cargo.toml/go.mod/Prisma schema (20 extractors). L2 instruction quality: vague language detection, positive/negative ratio, token budget with @file.md chain resolution. L3 context assembly: hook validation, skill frontmatter checks, rule glob validity, coverage ratio. ctxharness fix --apply auto-patches stale versions. ctxharness score gives 0-100 health score. ctxharness diff blocks CI on regression. GitHub Action available. Stack presets for T3, Next.js, Python, Go, Rust. v0.4.1. MIT. Made by RTK author.
Conflict / Overlap: No overlap with token-optimizer (complementary: token-optimizer finds unused/oversized skills; ctxharness finds factually stale content). No MCP server — CLI/CI tool only, zero agent-side conflicts.
Requirements: Node.js 18+; no model calls; npx ctxharness or npm install -g ctxharness
Activity (Issues / PRs): 2 forks, 3 releases, v0.4.1 May 2026, 58 commits, solo dev — authored by RTK maintainer
Verdict: Add — unique drift detection, complements token-optimizer
Decision Rule: Stack alongside token-optimizer: token-optimizer audits what is loaded, ctxharness audits whether what is loaded is still true. npx ctxharness scan CLAUDE.md for zero-config discovery. npx ctxharness init + ctxharness doctor for full enforcement. Add to CI: ctxharness diff exits 1 on score regression. 4 stars but authored by RTK maintainer (11k stars) — highly credible.

What it does: Local, zero-config observability dashboard (Next.js frontend + FastAPI backend) reading log files that AI coding agents already write to disk — no SDK, no instrumentation, no cloud account. Unified dashboard: token usage/cost per session-model-project, tool-call waterfall traces, reasoning chain capture, plan-mode capture, per-model efficiency comparison, per-project budget limits (observational, never blocks agents), cost anomaly detection (flags silent reasoning-token waste). Dedicated Hermes Agent dashboard (Nous Research autonomous-agent framework): gateway health, cron jobs, skills, memory, subagent delegation traces across 38 source platforms — the only tool in this comparison covering a non-coding autonomous agent. Reads logs from 10 coding agents: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Qwen CLI, OpenCode, Vibe, Antigravity, Grok Build. Separate Hermes Dashboard plugin for embedding as a tab. 67 commits, no formal releases yet.
Conflict / Overlap: Different kind of tool than token-optimizer/ctxharness (Config stack audit layer): purely observational/read-only, no config modification or active fixing, whereas those tools actively audit and patch. Complementary — use TokenTelemetry to see where tokens go, then token-optimizer/ctxharness to fix it. No MCP server, no MCP tool name conflicts.
Requirements: Node.js 18+, Python 3.9+, git. Zero-LLM — pure local log-file parsing, no API key needed. ⚠ Anonymous product-usage telemetry on by default (content-free: pages/features used, OS/CPU arch, approximate country, generated session id — never code/prompts/costs/paths). Opt out via Settings → Usage & privacy, or DO_NOT_TRACK=1 / TT_NO_TELEMETRY=1 env vars. curl -fsSL https://tokentelemetry.com/install.sh | bash.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 3 forks (per direct fetch; grown further per search), 67 commits, no releases published, very rapid feature growth in under 2 months of existence
Verdict: Add — unique cross-agent observability breadth, purely local
Decision Rule: ⚠ Direct page fetch showed a stale snapshot (20 stars, older README claiming "no telemetry"); live Issues page confirms 148 stars and the current shipped version explicitly discloses anonymous opt-out telemetry — the "no telemetry" claim in the fetched README is no longer accurate as a blanket statement (Code-Beats-Docs catch: verify against current shipped behaviour, not a cached README). Genuinely unique: only tool here unifying observability across 10 coding agents plus a non-coding autonomous agent (Hermes). Read-only — never modifies agent logs or config. RepoRadar automated scoring: Shipping Velocity 100/100, Activity Recency 100/100, but Production Readiness 26/100 and Project Maturity 32/100 — moving fast, still early.

What it does: Runtime contract enforcement for AI agents. Write rules in plain English; Sponsio compiles to LTL then DFA and checks every tool call in <0.01ms with zero LLM cost. Pre-built bundles covering all 10 OWASP Agentic Top 10 risks. Blocks credential-scope abuse, destructive API calls, runaway loops. Replays real incidents (PocketOS prod-DB wipe, CVE-2025-55284). Framework-agnostic: LangChain, Claude Agent, OpenAI Agents, MCP, CrewAI, Vercel AI. Python + TypeScript SDK. v0.1.0 alpha.
Conflict / Overlap: Out of scope for context reduction — different problem (agent safety vs token bloat). No overlap with any existing layer. Listed as separate layer for completeness.
Requirements: Python 3.9+ or Node.js 18+; no model calls; pip install sponsio or npm install sponsio
Activity (Issues / PRs): 14 open issues, 5 open PRs, v0.1.0 alpha, active development
Verdict: Watch — out of scope but unique
Decision Rule: Agent safety tool, not a context reduction tool. Belongs in a separate AI agent safety comparison (alongside Guardrails AI, Salus YC W26). Add if you need runtime enforcement of destructive-action gates (prod DB deletes, large transfers). Apache-2.0, pip install sponsio.

What it does: Indexes CSV/Excel files once into SQLite; agents query column profiles, filtered rows, and server-side aggregations instead of loading entire files. 9 MCP tools: index_local, describe_dataset, describe_column, search_data, get_rows (10 filter operators), aggregate (GROUP BY in SQLite), sample_rows, list_datasets, get_session_stats. Token savings telemetry per call. 25,333x reduction benchmark on 1M-row CSV (111M→3,849 tokens, measured). Incremental re-index on unchanged files (hash check). v0.1.1.
Conflict / Overlap: ⚠ index_local and list_datasets names clash with jdocmunch-mcp — agent cannot distinguish doc indexing from data indexing if both are loaded.
Requirements: Python 3.10+; no model calls; uvx jdatamunch-mcp
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 2 releases, v0.1.1 Mar 2026, 13 commits, solo dev, brand new
Verdict: Watch — too early, but genuinely unique layer
Decision Rule: Only tool in this comparison addressing large CSV/Excel token bloat. 25,333x benchmark is reproducible (LAPD 1M-row dataset). Same dual-use licence as jCodemunch/jDocMunch — free for personal/OSS, commercial use requires paid licence. uvx jdatamunch-mcp to install. Revisit when community forks appear.

What it does: Go agent runtime kernel. Dynamically loads only 3-5 tools per task (99.9% reduction: 60,468 tokens raw → ~80 tokens). 6-signal weighted scoring: relevance (40%), success rate (30%), cost (-15%), latency (-5%), confidence (10%), memory bonus. Online learning persists across restarts (ark-memory.json). Adaptive execution: tool-not-found → load more; tool-misunderstood → upgrade schema; tool-crashed → swap. Decision-level cost attribution per step/tool/action. Grounding gate (zero hallucination across 30-run stress test). Safety: domain allowlist, write protection, dry-run. 67 tests, race detector clean. v0.7 current. Go 1.22+, Apache-2.0.
Conflict / Overlap: No overlap with existing tools — new layer (agent runtime). Most conceptually adjacent to Tool Search and mcpick (MCP defs layer) but operates at higher level: active 6-signal scoring vs passive schema deferral. v0.8 roadmap adds MCP server connector which will enable direct Claude Code integration.
Requirements: Go 1.22+; no model calls in core; uses your configured LLM provider via agent.yaml; go run ./cmd/ark
Activity (Issues / PRs): 1 fork, 0 releases, 8 commits, v0.7 current, solo dev — brand new
Verdict: Watch — new layer, not Claude Code-native yet
Decision Rule: Not a Claude Code plugin today — standalone agent runtime requiring agent.yaml config. v0.8 roadmap adds MCP server connector enabling direct MCP stack integration. Most sophisticated tool scoring model in this comparison (6 signals including real dollar cost). Online learning unique. Revisit at v0.8 when MCP connector ships. go run ./cmd/ark demo-learn to see ranking in action.

What it does: Compresses everything before it reaches the LLM: tool outputs, logs, RAG chunks, files, conversation history. 7 algorithms: SmartCrusher (JSON), CodeCompressor (AST), Kompress-base (custom HuggingFace model), image compression, CacheAligner (stabilises KV cache prefixes), IntelligentContext, CCR (reversible — originals stored locally, LLM retrieves on demand). 4 deployment modes: headroom wrap claude, proxy (port 8787, zero code changes), library, MCP server (headroom_compress, headroom_retrieve, headroom_stats). Cross-agent memory. headroom learn: mines failed sessions, writes to CLAUDE.md. 60B+ community tokens saved. v0.22.4. 152 releases. Ships RTK internally.
Conflict / Overlap: ⚠ Subsumes multiple layers: ships RTK internally (remove RTK from stack if using headroom wrap); headroom learn overlaps openwolf cerebrum.md; cross-agent memory overlaps lean-ctx CCP and Bitloops; headroom_compress overlaps caveman. Do not run headroom alongside RTK + caveman + context-mode — triple redundancy.
Requirements: ⚠ Python 3.10+; Kompress-base model downloaded on first use (HuggingFace, ~100MB); [ml] extra required for model compression; ships RTK binary internally; no external API calls in default mode
Activity (Issues / PRs): 4k forks, 169 watching, 158 releases, v0.28.0 (29 Jun 2026), 1,829 commits — repo transferred from chopratejas/headroom to headroomlabs-ai org (same repository_id, confirmed same project). New "Headroom for teams" managed offering announced (hello@headroomlabs.ai), core stays Apache-2.0.
Verdict: Add — most comprehensive compression tool in this comparison
Decision Rule: ⚠ MAJOR: 42k stars (7.5x jump since last recording). Now #1 most-starred universal compression tool. | If using headroom wrap claude: remove RTK, caveman, context-mode from stack (headroom handles all three). If using headroom mcp install only: run alongside RTK for shell output, caveman for structured memory compression. pip install "headroom-ai[all]" then headroom wrap claude. CacheAligner uniquely stabilises KV cache prefixes — provider cost savings on top of token savings. 60B+ tokens saved community leaderboard at headroomlabs.ai/dashboard.

What it does: Pre-execution governance engine. Sits between agent and tools; evaluates every tool call against FPL policy (PERMIT/DEFER/DENY) via 11-step deterministic pipeline with no LLM in the decision path. faramesh mcp wrap intercepts every MCP tools/call before it reaches the real server. Credential broker strips API keys and mints short-lived scoped credentials per permitted call. Kernel enforcement on Linux (seccomp-BPF, Landlock, netns). SPIFFE/SPIRE workload identity. Tamper-evident WAL with Ed25519 signing. 13 framework auto-patches (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Claude Code, MCP, OpenAI Agents, etc). Policy packs. v1.2.6. arXiv 2601.17744.
Conflict / Overlap: No overlap with context reduction tools — different problem. Complements Sponsio (both agent safety; faramesh is broader: credentials, kernel enforcement, SPIFFE, multi-agent delegation). Context reduction angle: replacing prompt-based guardrails in CLAUDE.md with deterministic enforcement frees tokens currently used for safety reminders.
Requirements: Go binary; no model calls; brew install faramesh/tap/faramesh or go install. Faramesh Horizon is a paid tier above free Core.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 11 forks, 8 releases, v1.2.6 Apr 2026, 206 commits, 2 open issues, company-backed (Faramesh Labs)
Verdict: Add — broader than Sponsio; complements context reduction indirectly
Decision Rule: Deterministic runtime enforcement of safety rules means you can remove those rules from CLAUDE.md, reclaiming prompt tokens. faramesh mcp wrap governs every MCP tools/call — zero code changes required. arXiv 2601.17744 for technical architecture. Faramesh Horizon is paid tier for advanced features.

Compares Membase, OpenHuman, GBrain, Supermemory, Mem0/OpenMemory, Zep/Graphiti, Cognee, Khoj, Obsidian+AI bridge, ChatGPT Memory, Claude Projects, NotebookLM. Deep-dives: capability matrix, setup burden, agent activation channels, local vs cloud, personal vs team. 0 releases, 21 commits.

Covers research papers, engineering frameworks, system designs, evaluation benchmarks, real-world application practices. Maintained by IAAR-Shanghai (Institute for Advanced Algorithms Research, Shanghai) — an institutional AI research organization, not an anonymous account. 312 commits, no releases (rolling updates). Referenced by at least one other awesome-list as a primary source.

No disclosed commercial conflict of interest, unlike awesome-second-brain (aristoapp/Membase). Overlaps conceptually with the Agent memory persistence layer tools (mem0, cognee, claude-mem, TrueMemory, etc.) as a discovery resource for that category.

What it does: Category map for agent credential management: vaults, proxies, identity layers, credential gateways. Five reader-intent buckets: Products (vaults/proxies you install), Integrations (how Claude Code/Codex/Cursor/LangChain etc. consume credentials today), Services (per-API recipes: Stripe, GitHub, Slack scoping guidance), Patterns (named recipes: lethal trifecta, just-in-time injection, subagent non-inheritance), Threat models (prompt-injection credential disclosure, shell history exposure, offline disk-image theft). Compatibility matrix across 10 agent platforms x 15 vault products. Curated by Authsome (agentrhq) — a credential-vault vendor whose own product is listed among direct competitors. CC0 1.0 (public domain). "Last verified: 2026-05-27." 5 commits.
Conflict / Overlap: Reference list only — not installable. Different topic from this comparison's core scope (credential/secrets management, not context/token reduction) — same adjacent-but-included treatment as Sponsio and faramesh-core in the Agent safety enforcement layer. faramesh-core (already in this comparison) appears as an entry in this list too, confirming the overlap.
Requirements: Not installable; no setup required.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 5 commits, no releases, brand new — genuinely tiny but unusually well-structured
Verdict: Reference only — not a tool; adjacent layer (agent credential security, not context reduction)

Notably strong conflict-of-interest handling: maintainer's own product (Authsome) is listed alongside direct competitors with an explicit stated policy — "Direct competitors of the maintainer get accepted on equal terms." Stronger COI safeguard than most reference resources in this comparison. Use if evaluating how to hand credentials to Claude Code/Codex/Cursor safely — separate concern from token/context reduction.

What it does: Comprehensive curated list for the entire discipline of AI agent harness engineering: the scaffolding (context delivery, tool interfaces, planning artifacts, verification loops, memory systems, sandboxes) surrounding an agent. Sections: Foundations (canonical essays from Anthropic/OpenAI/Google/Martin Fowler on what harness engineering is), Agent Loop, Planning & Task Decomposition, Context Delivery & Compaction (directly cites context-mode, headroom, codebase-memory-mcp — tools already in this comparison — plus many not yet assessed here: LLMLingua, Token Savior, Trellis, OpenViking, Mirage, dirac, MinishLab/semble), Tool Design, Skills & MCP, Permissions & Authorization, Memory & State (Letta/MemGPT, mem0, Zep, engram, Stash, TencentDB-Agent-Memory), Task Runners & Orchestration, Verification & CI, Observability, Debugging, Human-in-the-Loop, Reference Implementations, Security/Sandbox, Evals, Templates. Substantive one-paragraph annotation per entry, not bare links. 166 commits, 81 open PRs — very active community contribution.
Conflict / Overlap: Reference list only — not installable. Directly overlaps this comparison's entire scope rather than one layer, unlike awesome-second-brain or Awesome-AI-Memory. Several entries here (context-mode, headroom, codebase-memory-mcp, mem0) are already independently assessed in this comparison; this list surfaces a substantial number of additional candidate tools not yet evaluated here.
Requirements: Not installable; no setup required.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 284 forks, 166 commits, 4 open issues, 81 open PRs, no releases (rolling updates) — very active
Verdict: Reference only — not a tool; broadest and most directly relevant reference resource in this comparison
Decision Rule: Not installable. The most comprehensive and directly on-topic reference resource assessed for this comparison — spans context compaction, memory, tool/MCP design, and permissions rather than one narrow layer. Surfaces genuine candidate tools not yet in this comparison worth following up on individually: LLMLingua (Microsoft prompt compression, up to 20x), Token Savior (symbol-indexed navigation, 77% token cut), Trellis (progressive spec system replacing bloated CLAUDE.md), OpenViking (ByteDance filesystem-paradigm context DB), engram (Go+SQLite+FTS5 memory, 18 MCP tools), Zep (production memory store). Not acted on in this pass — flagged for future individual assessment.

What it does: Universal memory layer for AI agents. Extracts atomic facts from conversations and persists them scoped to user, session, or agent. On next session injects relevant memories — agent knows your preferences and history without re-reading full conversation history. Native .claude-plugin and .cursor-plugin integrations. OpenMemory: free self-hosted Docker path. MCP server. Python + TypeScript SDKs. 277 releases, v1.0.9. arXiv:2504.19413.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts with existing tools in this comparison. Overlaps supermemory and cognee (all three are agent memory persistence — different power/complexity tradeoff). Do not run all three simultaneously — pick one.
Requirements: ⚠ Requires LLM API key to extract memories (OpenAI default; supports Claude, Gemini, Groq, Ollama). pip install mem0ai. OpenMemory self-hosted: Docker one-liner (free, no paid tier needed for basic use). Graph features (Mem0g) require $249/mo Pro tier.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 56.5k stars (was 51.4k), $24M funded, weekly releases, known LongMemEval temporal gap
Verdict: Add — largest community of any memory tool; 90% token reduction vs full-context
Decision Rule: Known limitation: 49% LongMemEval on temporal sub-tasks vs 63-91% for Zep/graphiti alternatives. | 51.4k stars, native Claude Code plugin. Benchmarks: +26% accuracy, 91% faster, 90% fewer tokens vs full-context (LOCOMO benchmark). Telemetry: set MEM0_TELEMETRY=false to disable. For self-host without paid tier: run OpenMemory with docker-compose up in the openmemory/ dir.

What it does: Knowledge graph memory engine. Four operations: remember, recall, forget, improve. Dual layer: fast session cache + persistent graph with background sync. Claude Code plugin: captures tool calls into session memory via hooks, syncs to graph at session end. cognee-mcp developer rules bootstrap: indexes .cursorrules, .cursor/rules, AGENT.md into developer_rules nodeset — queryable rather than loaded wholesale. MCP server with HTTP/SSE/stdio transports. ~12k stars, v1.1.0-dev.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts. Overlaps mem0 and supermemory (all agent memory persistence). cognee builds a full knowledge graph; mem0 stores atomic facts; supermemory is SaaS-hosted. Pick one for the memory persistence layer.
Requirements: ⚠ Requires LLM API key for knowledge graph extraction (OpenAI default; supports Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, Ollama). pip install cognee. Docker for self-hosting. Telemetry on by default — disable via environment variable.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 17.5k stars (was 12k), graduated GitHub Secure Open Source Program, FalkorDB/KuzuDB/Neo4j backends
Verdict: Add — knowledge graph memory with Claude Code plugin + developer rules bootstrap
Decision Rule: Graduated GitHub Secure Open Source Program. | Claude Code plugin: /plugin marketplace add topoteretes/cognee or git clone + install.sh. developer rules bootstrap indexes CLAUDE.md equivalents into queryable graph rather than loading wholesale at startup. More powerful than mem0 (graph reasoning) but heavier setup. For simple session memory mem0 is easier.

What it does: SaaS-hosted memory API for AI agents. Dedicated Claude Code plugin: injects user profile (preferences, recent activity) at session start; auto-captures conversation turns for future recall. MCP server at mcp.supermemory.ai. Scoped by project container tags. Team Memory across repos. Browser extension. Also plugins for OpenCode, OpenClaw, Hermes. npm install supermemory or pip install supermemory. Personal use free.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts. Overlaps mem0 and cognee (all agent memory persistence). Supermemory is the simplest path (zero infra, SaaS) but data stored on Supermemory servers. mem0 OpenMemory is the self-hosted alternative.
Requirements: ⚠ SaaS-hosted — memory stored on Supermemory servers (not local). API key required (sm_...). Personal use free; Team Memory requires Pro (paid). npm install supermemory or /plugin marketplace add supermemoryai/claude-supermemory.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 2.6k stars (confirmed stable), 151 forks, active multi-platform ecosystem (Codex, OpenClaw, Cursor plugins)
Verdict: Add — simplest path to Claude Code session memory; zero infra
Decision Rule: Simplest of the three memory tools: SaaS, no local infra, one install. /plugin marketplace add supermemoryai/claude-supermemory. Tradeoff: data lives on Supermemory servers. For self-hosted memory use mem0 OpenMemory instead. Team Memory needs paid plan.

What it does: Temporal context graph engine. Every fact has a validity window — when it became true and when superseded. Agents can query "what was true on Tuesday" not just "what is true now". Incremental graph construction (no batch recomputation). Hybrid retrieval: semantic + BM25 + graph traversal. mcp_server/ directory for Claude Code and Cursor integration. 194 releases, v0.29.0 Apr 2026. arXiv:2501.13956. Backends: Neo4j, FalkorDB (Docker), KùzuDB, Amazon Neptune.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts with existing tools. Overlaps Bitloops and Aura (cross-session governance) but graphiti is more production-grade and specifically optimised for temporal reasoning. Complementary to mem0 (different problem: temporal history vs current facts).
Requirements: ⚠ Requires graph database backend: Neo4j, FalkorDB (docker run -p 6379:6379 falkordb/falkordb:latest), KùzuDB, or Amazon Neptune. Requires LLM API key (OpenAI default; Anthropic/Gemini/Groq/Ollama supported). pip install graphiti-core. Telemetry opt-out: GRAPHITI_TELEMETRY_ENABLED=false.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 20k+ stars, Apache-2.0, self-hostable but requires Neo4j. LongMemEval temporal: 63.8% vs mem0 49%
Verdict: Watch — powerful but infra-heavy; best for temporal reasoning about evolving facts
Decision Rule: Not zero-config. Best for: architecture decisions that evolve over time, API contracts that change, "what did we decide about auth and when did that change?" FalkorDB is the lowest-friction backend (single Docker command). Works best with OpenAI/Gemini structured output; Anthropic supported but less reliable.

What it does: Claude Code skill (installed to ~/.claude/skills/) that turns your Obsidian vault into a persistent second brain. 43 commands. /obsidian-architect (v0.10, May 2026): scans your codebase and writes maintained architecture notes into your vault — refreshable, never overwrites your edits. /obsidian-save, /obsidian-research, /obsidian-recall, /sunday weekly review, /log capture. AI-first vault design: machine-readable frontmatter, mandatory wikilinks, recency markers optimised for Claude retrieval. Google Calendar integration. Anti-hallucination guards. 1,000+ stars in 7 weeks.

What it does: Git-native shared memory for AI coding agents. Memory = markdown files in git repos, synced through GitHub, governed via PRs. Fixes the multi-tool problem: Claude Code + Codex + Copilot + Gemini + OpenCode share the same memory. Encoding strength 1-5 (AST-parsed fact S:4 outranks LLM inference S:2 — hard rule not scoring). Dream pipeline: orient, gather, consolidate, lint, prune on free LLM quota. PR governance: cheap models propose, SotA models review, humans confirm. Provenance tracking. Cognitive science taxonomy (19 references in spec). Zero infrastructure — pure git + markdown.

Branch-aware memory: git checkout to feature branch → agent uses branch-appropriate memories, no experimental patterns bleeding into production. Solves token rent: targeted injection preserves prefix cache vs CLAUDE.md global invalidation. memoir blame/checkout for auditing. Cryptographic integrity. Visual explorer (Tree/Graph/Timeline/Places). Native Claude Code plugin via .claude-plugin/. Hooks for session-start, user-prompt-submit, stop. v0.1.9. 145 commits.

What it does: Dual-graph context engine wrapping AI agent invocations. Run dgc instead of claude. Builds info_graph.json (semantic graph: files, symbols, imports) + chat_action_graph.json (session memory: reads, edits, queries). Pre-loads relevant files per prompt — agent reasons, not explores. Compounding context: each turn cheaper as graph fills in. MCP tools: graph_read, graph_retrieve, graph_neighbors, count_tokens, get_session_stats. Token dashboard at localhost:8899. Cross-session context-store.json. Supports Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Copilot. v3.9.90 (rapid PyPI versioning). Benchmarks: $0.46→$0.27 (41% cheaper), 16.8→10.3 turns, 82.7→87.1 quality across 80+ prompts.
Conflict / Overlap: ⚠ graph_read/graph_retrieve overlap conceptually with codegraph/codebase-memory-mcp but different MCP tool names — low hard-conflict risk. Running GrapeRoot alongside another code nav MCP is redundant (both pre-load codebase context). Pick one code nav approach.
Requirements: ⚠ Core graph engine (graperoot PyPI package) is PROPRIETARY — launcher is Apache 2.0 but the intelligence that does the actual work is closed-source. Python 3.10+, Node.js 18+, Claude Code or Codex CLI. curl one-liner install. No self-host of full stack possible.

Launcher Apache 2.0 is misleading — the core is not open source.

What it does: Claude Code-native persistent memory. 5 lifecycle hooks (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd) capture tool-use observations during sessions, compress via Claude agent SDK, store in SQLite + Chroma vector DB. Worker service: HTTP API port 37777, web viewer UI, 10 search endpoints, managed by Bun. mem-search skill for natural language queries with progressive disclosure. 4 MCP tools in 3-layer workflow: search (compact index, ~50-100 tokens/result) → timeline (chronological context) → get_observations (full details, ~500-1000 tokens/result, only for filtered IDs) — ~10x token savings vs fetching everything upfront. Privacy: <private> tags exclude content from storage. Beta channel: Endless Mode (biomimetic memory for extended sessions). Multi-language modes (zh, ja). Works with Claude Code, OpenClaw, Codex, Gemini, Hermes, Copilot, OpenCode. v13.2.0, 270 releases.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts with existing tools. Overlaps mem0/cognee/supermemory/gitmem/memoir (all agent memory persistence) — claude-mem is the most Claude Code-native and by far the most adopted (77k vs mem0 51.4k). Pick one memory tool.
Requirements: ⚠ Compression step uses Claude agent SDK (calls Claude API/session). Auto-installs Bun and uv (Python, for vector search) if missing. Node.js 18+. npx claude-mem install or /plugin marketplace add thedotmack/claude-mem — npm install -g alone does NOT register hooks.

Install via npx claude-mem install, not npm install -g.

What it does: Self-improvement plugin for Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode that turns interactions into durable skills. Distinction from memory tools: LEARNING (actionable rules Claude follows) not MEMORY (transcript of what happened). Three artifacts auto-built: Preferences (project-scoped, how you work here), Project-specific skills (triggered rules with rationales), Shared skills (cross-project rollups). Self-tuning library: correct same thing twice → rules merge; change mind → old archived, new supersedes; near-duplicates consolidate. Hybrid vector + BM25 search via in-process ONNX embedder (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, ~86MB, no external API). Dashboard at localhost:3001. Citation markers in replies. /claude-smart:learn, /show, /dashboard commands. Powered by Reflexio engine (separate Apache-2.0 project). 45 tags, v0.2.47. Benchmarked vs claude-mem: ~2.7x higher overall quality, ~3x better correction retention (EXPERIMENT.md). GDPVal benchmark on Reflexio engine: -81% planning steps, -72% tokens.
Conflict / Overlap: ⛔ Direct competitor to claude-mem — README benchmarks explicitly against it, do not run both. Overlaps mem0/cognee/TrueMemory/gitmem/memoir (all agent memory persistence — pick one fact-memory tool). ACE (agentic-context-engine) is stackable (ACE = strategy memory, claude-smart = skill/rule memory — different abstractions).
Requirements: Python 3.12+, Node.js 20+, uv (Python package manager). ONNX model (~86MB, downloaded once on first use). No external API for core embeddings — runs in-process. No LLM API needed for extraction (uses local model). npx claude-smart install then restart Claude Code. Or: claude plugin marketplace add ReflexioAI/claude-smart.

Reflexio engine also has standalone uses beyond claude-smart. CONFLICT: do not run alongside claude-mem — direct competitors.

What it does: Local-first agent memory in one SQLite file. Three tiers: Edge (fastest, runs anywhere), Base (strongest fully-offline, 88.2% LoCoMo), Pro (adds HyDE query expansion via LLM API, 93.0% LoCoMo). Lifecycle hooks auto-capture conversations; memories processed/consolidated on session end ("dreaming"). Directives: m.add(content, directive=True) for standing instructions auto-loaded every session — a per-memory CLAUDE.md alternative. search_deep: agentic multi-round search for ambiguous queries. Benchmarked on LoCoMo (1540 q), LongMemEval (500 q), and BEAM-1M/BEAM-10M (novel: tests memory recall across conversations exceeding any model context window, up to 10M tokens / ~20,000 messages). Works with Claude Code, Claude CLI, Cursor, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Claude Desktop. Rebranded from "Neuromem". v0.7.1.2+, hyperactive releases.

What it does: Continual learning framework based on Stanford/SambaNova ACE paper and Dynamic Cheatsheet. Maintains a persistent "Skillbook" of learned strategies (not declarative facts) that functions as an evolving system prompt. Three roles: Agent (executes, enhanced by Skillbook), Reflector (analyzes traces — writes/executes Python in sandboxed env to programmatically find patterns, the "Recursive Reflector"), SkillManager (curates Skillbook). No fine-tuning, no training data, no vector database. Runners: ACELiteLLM (any of 100+ LiteLLM providers), Core (batch epochs), TraceAnalyser (learn from existing logs), browser-use, LangChain, ClaudeCode (Claude Code CLI tasks with learning). Optional MCP server (ace-framework[mcp]) and embedding-based deduplication. Composable pipeline: AgentStep→EvaluateStep→ReflectStep→UpdateStep→DeduplicateStep. Optional hosted SaaS at kayba.ai (free trial). v0.12.0, 32 releases.
Conflict / Overlap: Different sub-category within Agent memory persistence: stores learned STRATEGIES (procedural, "this approach worked for this task type") not declarative FACTS like mem0/cognee/supermemory/claude-mem/gitmem/memoir/TrueMemory (those store "user prefers X"). Complementary rather than directly competing — could run alongside a fact-memory tool.
Requirements: ⚠ Requires LLM API key for every learning step (Reflector + SkillManager), no offline tier — 100+ providers via LiteLLM. Recursive Reflector executes Python code in a sandboxed environment. uv add ace-framework, then ace setup for interactive config.

Based on a real arXiv paper (2510.04618). Has a dedicated ClaudeCode runner class for direct Claude Code integration. Optional hosted dashboard at kayba.ai is freemium, not required.

What it does: Daily journal built on the "latent memory" thesis: LLM weights already encode general world knowledge, so the tool only needs to store the tiny cues the model cannot invent — your specific decisions and why. One plain-markdown file per day (~/mem/journal/YYYY-MM-DD.md), no database, no cloud, no vector embeddings, no LLM extraction step. Journal is shared across ALL projects by default (project: prefix per line), not siloed per-repo. Commands: mem log, mem task/tasks/done, mem last 7 (restore a week + open tasks), mem search, mem edit. Auto-commits every write to git as a lightweight backup. Piped command output auto-trimmed to cue + evidence (e.g. pytest 2>&1 | mem log "myapp: tests: 3 fail, all JWT expiry"). Installs Claude/Grok/AGENTS.md instruction snippets + a SKILL.md (also mirrored at skills/mem/ for skill-marketplace discovery) during setup. Tri-platform from day one: PowerShell 31.5% / Python 31.1% / Shell 31.0%.
Conflict / Overlap: Overlaps the fact-memory cluster in this layer (mem0/cognee/claude-mem/TrueMemory/supermemory/gitmem/memoir/claude-smart) — pick one. Closest in spirit to gitmem (git-backed, markdown, zero infra) but simpler: gitmem still runs an LLM "dream pipeline" for consolidation and has PR governance; mem has neither. No MCP server — CLI + AGENTS.md/SKILL.md instruction pattern.
Requirements: Zero-LLM; no model calls anywhere — pure structured markdown read/write. Python 3.8+ and git required. curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karmugilen/mem/main/setup.sh | bash (macOS/Linux) or irm .../setup.ps1 | iex (Windows).
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 4 commits, no releases published — genuinely day-one, no third-party coverage found anywhere
Verdict: Watch — too early, but a clean minimalist counterpoint to increasingly complex memory tools
Decision Rule: Zero LLM calls, zero infrastructure, zero cost — the most minimal design in the entire Agent memory persistence layer, useful as a baseline to compare against heavier tools like cognee or graphiti. Cross-project single journal (not per-repo) is a genuine differentiator most other memory tools in this comparison lack. Honest self-caveat in the README about auto-commit: "a backup you must remember isn't a backup." Worth revisiting once any community signal appears — currently zero adoption evidence.

What it does: Deliberately minimal project-local memory MCP server. Entire public API is two frozen tools: remember(text) stores a piece of project memory, recall(text) retrieves relevant pieces — no kinds, states, filters, or knobs. README states explicitly: "the two tools, their strict input schemas, and the public text/structuredContent surfaces are stable and frozen." Raw input is never persisted — a controller-normalization + safety-filter layer runs before write; only normalized summaries and metadata (kind, confidence, safety flags, timestamps) land in local SQLite (.curion/curion.sqlite, gitignored). stdio transport only — curion opens no network sockets itself; the MCP host owns the network boundary as subprocess spawn. Opt-in semantic retrieval can search across other non-private projects on the same machine; a project marked isPrivate:true in .curion/config.json is never surfaced to cross-project search. Pluggable provider: OpenAI-compatible (default) or Anthropic. Does not load .env files — config passed via parent process env only. Full docs tree (mcp-clients, configuration, reference, privacy-storage), SECURITY.md, CHANGELOG (Keep a Changelog format). 4 releases, v0.3.6 (Jun 30 2026), 71 commits.
Conflict / Overlap: Overlaps the fact-memory cluster (mem0/cognee/claude-mem/TrueMemory/supermemory/gitmem/memoir/claude-smart/mem) — pick one. No hard MCP tool name collision (remember/recall not used elsewhere in this comparison). Most minimal public API surface of any memory tool here — explicit design commitment to NOT expand it, contrasted with most competitors actively adding tools release over release.
Requirements: ⚠ Extraction-only: requires LLM API key (OpenAI-compatible default, or Anthropic) for controller-normalization step — CURION_PRIMARY_API_KEY/BASE_URL/MODEL. Node.js >= 22. npm install -g @geanatz/curion, then claude mcp add --scope project --transport stdio curion -- curion.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 4 releases, v0.3.6 Jun 30 2026, 71 commits — genuinely early, no third-party coverage found anywhere
Verdict: Watch — too early, but disciplined design (frozen minimal API, safety-filtered writes)
Decision Rule: Explicit API-freeze commitment at v0.3.6 is unusual — most memory tools in this comparison are still expanding their tool surface. Raw-input-never-persisted design (normalize + safety-filter before write, reject silently drops the call) is a distinctive privacy mechanism not described by any other memory tool here. Cross-project semantic search with a per-project private opt-out is a genuinely useful default for anyone running curion across multiple repos on one machine. Contrast with mem (karmugilen/mem): both minimal, but mem is zero-LLM/zero-cost while curion needs an API key for its normalization layer in exchange for safety filtering and semantic recall.

What it does: Skill/plugin enforcing a YAGNI decision ladder before the agent writes any code: (1) does this need to exist — skip if not, (2) does stdlib do it, (3) native platform feature, (4) installed dependency, (5) one line, (6) only then the minimum that works. Never cuts validation, error handling, security, or accessibility. Commands: /ponytail [lite|full|ultra|off], /ponytail-review (diff over-engineering check), /ponytail-audit (whole-repo audit), /ponytail-debt (harvest deferred shortcuts into a ledger), /ponytail-gain (benchmark scoreboard). 14 platforms: Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, Pi, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, CodeWhale, OpenClaw, plus instruction-only Cursor/Windsurf/Cline/Kiro. Agentic benchmark (real Claude Code session, real FastAPI+React repo, 12 tasks, n=4): -54% LOC, -22% tokens, -20% cost, -27% time vs no-skill baseline, 100% safe. v4.7.0, 9 releases, created 12 Jun 2026.
Conflict / Overlap: No MCP tool name conflicts (skill-based, not an MCP server). Different mechanism from caveman (Response & memory compression): caveman compresses output/CLAUDE.md after the fact; ponytail prevents over-engineered code from being generated in the first place. Direct benchmark comparison included: caveman increased tokens/cost/time in their controlled test while ponytail decreased all three — different tools solving different problems, not a refutation.
Requirements: No model calls of its own — runs inside the host agent. Claude Code/Codex hooks require node on PATH. /plugin marketplace add DietrichGebert/ponytail (Claude Code) or platform-specific install (14 platforms supported). No config required; PONYTAIL_DEFAULT_MODE env var optional.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 4.4k forks, 81.8k confirmed via GitHubVC live trending tracker, corroborated by findarepo (~79-80k) and OpenGithubs weekly (80.7k) — massive continued growth since Jun 18 benchmark update
Verdict: Add — distinctive mechanism, exceptional velocity, very young

Multiple independent sources confirm organic growth (YouTube Dev Tools Weekly, EveryDev.ai, OpenAgentSkill audit: 96/100 security, 100/100 maintenance) — not a star-farm pattern. Notable intellectual honesty: README explicitly corrects an earlier inflated 80-94% single-shot benchmark after a GitHub issue (#126) pointed out it compared against a padded conversational baseline; current agentic benchmark is the defensible number. /plugin marketplace add DietrichGebert/ponytail then /plugin install ponytail@ponytail.

What it does: Local proxy + CLI sitting between Claude Code and any LLM provider. cf wrap claude starts proxy, indexes repo (Tree-sitter AST: JS/TS/TSX/Python/Go/Rust/Java), launches Claude Code through it. Protocol translation lets Claude Code drive Ollama, OpenAI, Groq, Gemini — not just Anthropic. Repository Graph: find_symbol, analyze_impact, find_route (SQLite + HNSW vector index). Context Optimizer: tool-schema minimization, keep-newest dedup (SimHash near-dup detection), age-gated AST skeletonization, junk vaulting (lockfiles, minified bundles, base64 blobs). Surgical AST-aware patching returns unified diff (no re-read to verify). Per-workspace persistent session memory (HNSW + SQLite). Live dashboard at localhost:3000. cf doctor: 8-point install diagnosis with --fix. Prebuilt native binaries for 5 platforms. v1.0.0 released 4 Jul 2026, 23 commits.
Conflict / Overlap: Overlaps headroom (both wrap the full request pipeline: proxy + compression). Overlaps code nav layer via its Repository Graph (find_symbol similar to codegraph/CodeGraphContext). Overlaps MCP definition tokens (schema minimization similar to Tool Search). Naming collision: at least 3 unrelated projects share "ContextForge" name (IBM/mcp-context-forge, contextforge-org/contextforge-cli, webdevtodayjason/context-forge) — verify repo URL carefully.
Requirements: Node.js 20.0.0+. Prebuilt binaries for Windows/macOS(Intel+ARM)/Linux(x64+ARM) — no compiler needed via npm install. ~23MB ONNX embedding model auto-downloaded on first run (SHA-256 verified). No LLM API key required for local-only use via Ollama. Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini keys only needed if routing to those providers. npm i -g @anuj612/contextforge then cf wrap claude.
Activity (Issues / PRs): 0 forks, 23 commits, v1.0.0 released 4 Jul 2026 (yesterday) — brand new, no third-party coverage yet
Verdict: Watch — released yesterday, 0 stars, but local-model-first design is genuinely differentiated
Decision Rule: Only tool in this comparison making Claude Code a first-class local-model client — cf wrap claude lets you keep the Claude Code UX while routing to a fully local Ollama model, zero Anthropic API key needed. Benchmark is a single honest case study (66% fewer round-trips, 72.8% fewer input tokens vs passthrough), not yet a full suite — SWE-bench runs explicitly listed as in-development in the roadmap. Disclosed known issue: token savings can be over-reported on failed upstream requests (e.g. bad API key) since compression is logged regardless of whether the request succeeded. Claude Code fully supported; Codex/Gemini CLI experimental. Revisit once community adoption and the promised benchmark suite land.
